title: The arrival in Abya Yala (America)

maps.text: Image of Asia continent, with the regions of Abya Yala (America), Africa, Europe, and Australia also represented
Image of Abya Yala (America) continent, highlighting the central and north reigion —or only the north, according to literatures—
Image of Abya Yala (America) continent, highlighting the South region

common.text:
### Introduction

Thousands of years ago, Socrates concluded something that is cited in different texts from *History Tracers* (<htcite0>; <htcite1>):
• "I neither know, nor think I know"

Reflection 1: This is very profound, except...

The recognition that knowledge is gigantic and that we know little, expressed by Socrates' phrase, is very beautiful. This is not the only way it presents itself to the population; it is also mentioned more directly: “I only know that I know nothing.”

Suppose there is something you have believed for many years, even though not a single piece of evidence has ever emerged to support it. If a new discovery could show that your conclusion is inaccurate and that there is something beyond it, how would you behave?

#### The origin

The origin of the peoples of *Abya Yala* (America) has already been studied in History Tracers through a proposal by José de Acosta who, as early as <htdate0>, suggested that the arrival of indigenous peoples would have occurred via Asia (**Figure 4**) (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Centuries after the initial proposal, we can confirm José de Acosta's conclusion not only by the appearance of indigenous peoples from north to south of the continent, but also thanks to the collection of genetic data in Asia and in Abya Yala, which confirmed these connections (<htcite2>; <htcite3>; <htcie4>; <htcite5>).
Figure 4: A map showing Indigenous migration routes across the Bering Strait. Photo taken at the Museo Nacional de Antropología en San Salvador, El Salvador (), on .
common.imgdesc: This image illustrates the various migrations from Asia, with peoples moving from the far north of the continent to the south.

common.text:
#### The Clovis case

For about 50 years, it was widely accepted that people inhabited the Clovis at actual region of the United States of America around <htdate0> and <htdate1>, and that these people would be ancestors of all the inhabitants of *Abya Yala* (America) (<htcite0>; <htcite1>). However, new data emerged and challenged this understanding. A fact that leads us, once again, to reflection:

source.text: Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.

common.text:
Reflection 2: What now?

If for 50 years you have received a piece of information, and new discoveries modify that data, what would your reaction be?

Would you study the new proposal before reaching a conclusion?

Would you automatically say it is false, because you liked the previous one so much that you would teach it to others?

Would you accept the new proposal and then affirm that the other one was wrong?

The study of the “Clovis first” model and the new findings are presented below, from the perspective of people who possibly lived in that place. These people will be presented generically, with matriarchal lineages, for reasons that will be described throughout the text.

prerequisites: <htcite0>: A general overview of how migration to America happened.

history.text:
#### The initial understanding

Our initial understanding of the arrival of the first populations in *Abya Yala* (America) is directly linked to the extinction of a megafauna that lived on the continent and which, among other animals, included the presence of mammoths (**Figure 5**). However, the period of human arrival is not a unanimous conclusion.
Figure 5: Mammoth fossil exhibited in Mexico. Photograph taken on , during a visit to the National Museum of Anthropology in Mexico ().
history.imgdesc: Nesta imagem vemos a parte debaixo do maxiliar de um mamute extindo a milhares de anos.

history.text:
#### The “Clovis first” proposal

The settlement proposal called “Clovis first” tells us that people living in the Clovis region built tools from bones, stones, and points, like the one shown in **Figure 6** exhibited at the British Museum, spreading first through the current territory of the United States and then southward into the continent (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

Evidence supporting this movement includes artifacts found from Mexico to Venezuela (<htcite2>).

source.text: Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.
Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.

history.text: Figure 6: Clovis point projectile exhibited at the British Museum. © Trustees of the British Museum ( ).
In addition to the material evidence, genetic expansion models show that a single migratory population could have occurred and expanded across the continent (<htcite0>).

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
Reflection 3: Everything solved?

We have an archaeological model that possesses data collected from different parts of the continent.

We have a genetic model that shows that just one migration across the entire continent is possible.

Therefore, we conclude that if we put the two together, we will solve all the questions about the arrival of humans in the Americas Abya Yala (America).

Can you imagine scenarios that could invalidate the previous conclusion?

sources.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text: In addition to the tools and genetic data already discussed earlier (<htcite0>; <htcite1>), we understand that, in addition to the megafauna extinction, indigenous people possibly carried out migrations in groups until they began to inhabit a specific region (<htcite2>).

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.
Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.

history.text: The existence of Clovis culture tools in different locations in the United States indirectly proves the existence of this person (<htcite0>).

source.text: Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.

history.text: The existence of Clovis culture tools in different locations in the United States indirectly proves the existence of this person (<htcite0>).

source.text: Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.

history.text:
#### Location

Just like the original Clovis culture, the Folsom and Dalton sites are also located in the north of the *Abya Yala* (America) continent and are known for the artifacts found at the site (<htcite0>).

#### Antiquity

Regarding the antiquity of the population of these sites, it can be inferred thanks to the bones of extinct bison found in the region, demonstrating that the culture existed from at least <htdate0>. These data also confirm that the population focused on hunting activity (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

sources.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
#### The haplogroup

Although we have not found a specific mention of the haplogroup of the inhabitants of the Folsom region, we follow the conclusion of the study by Fagundes, N. J. R. *et al* to assign haplogroup B2 to this person (<htcite0>).

#### Tools

In Folsom, various points were also found that, due to their shape, were probably produced in series, in a standardized manner, to be incorporated into local tools (<htcite1>).

An evolution of tools is also observed in the Folsom region, such as the appearance of possible stone knives and the use of *atlatls* (<htcite2>; <htcite3>). Esta evolución nos lleva a otra reflexión.

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text: Figure 7: Ornamental profile of a warrior. © The Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York ( ).

source.page: Bequest of Jane Costello Goldberg, from the Collection of Arnold I

history.imgdesc: Nesta figura, temoss um Guerreiro Moche segurando um atlat em sua mão direita.

history.text:
Reflection 4: One more irrefutable proof, until someone refutes it

The oldest estimated period in the Folsom excavations for the Paleoindians is between and , although there is still presence of more recent clay and organic matter ().

Between and , the Moche culture in Peru also used atlatls as shown in the hands of a warrior in Figure 7 ().

Upon reading these two pieces of information, did you come to the conclusion that the Moche culture learned from or inherited elements from the Folsom culture?

If so, did you take into account the more than 4,000 years between them?

Did you take into account that, although we show a Moche atlatl, at no point did we present a Folsom atlatl for comparison?

Did you consider that different peoples could have reached the same conclusion independently due to the long time interval?

source.page: Bequest of Jane Costello Goldberg, from the Collection of Arnold I

history.text: Easy answers to complex problems tend to present wrong solutions, although in some scenarios they might be correct. In this study, with the data we have presented, it would not be possible to categorically affirm a relationship between the Moche and the Folsom.
The existence of tools and animal bones confirms the existence of groups of people (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).
The existence of tools and animal bones confirms the existence of groups of people (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

#### At this moment

So far in this text, we have studied two archaeological sites located in what we call North America (**Figure 2**). We have seen that, based on them, there are grounds for, if similar but more recent elements are found, we can associate the origin of the peoples who live and came to South America as direct descendants of them, through genetics and tools with specific shapes (<htcite0>). However, let's reflect once more before we proceed.

Reflection 5: Eureka!

We are beginning to study the south of the Abya Yala (América) continent. With what we have seen so far, what do you think we will focus on in the next stage of the study?

Do you think we will confirm everything that has been studied?

Or do you think something new, which will overturn the previous understanding, will be shown?

#### The environment

Between approximately <htdate0> and <htdate1>, in the north of the continent, melting ice created conditions for movement along the continental coasts. In the south, in northern Patagonia, transformations also occurred, with the soil beginning to stabilize and the drainage regime changing from meltwater to low-energy rains, which would lead to the emergence of open forests around <htdate2> (<htcite0>; <htcite1>).

#### Tools

Having an environment that allows migration does not necessarily mean that it is possible to migrate through it. There is a need to find tools, fossils, and artificial cuts that allow us to identify that humans were at the site.

Research carried out by Dillehay, T. D. *et al.* shows that there are four types of stone materials in Monte Verde (**Figure 8**) over time, of which we highlight the following (<htcite2>):
- The oldest artifacts possibly date from <htdate3> and would be materials like well-rounded “slingstones” (**Figure 8c -d**) and flakes that were possibly not used as cultural artifacts (**Figure 8b**).
- Between approximately <htdate4> and <htdate5>, it is already possible to find chipped percussion stones (**Figure 8e**) and a spherical stone.
- Between approximately <htdate6> and <htdate7>, we have a basalt flake with clear percussion marks.
- Finally, between <htdate8> and <htdate9>, we have sets of materials clearly of cultural use, such as multifaceted wedges (**Figure 8h**).

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text: Figure 8: Stone tools found in Monte Verde. © Dillehay, T. D. *et al* (). Shared under Open Access license.

Reflection 6: This reminds me...

When looking at Figure 8 and comparing it with the points found in Clovis and Folsom, can you see similarities?

And regarding the differences?

Do they remind you of any possible tool?

sources.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
#### From north to south

Due to the great distance between the north and south of the *Abya Yala* (América) continent scholars estimate that the trajectory, in ancient times, between the extremes of the continent would have taken between 600 and 1000 years (<htcite0>).

In addition to the time needed to carry out the migration, another factor to consider is the possible trajectories used to make the crossing. There would be at least two possible paths: along the coasts or through the interior of the continent (<htcite1>). In both cases, due to the climatic conditions associated with the period in which the migrations occurred, it is unlikely that anything is visible today.

#### Diet

Organic remains are found at the Monte Verde site from at least <htdate0>. Among these organic remains we find aquatic plants, plants originating from the forest, and microalgae. Besides food, plants possibly used as medicinal treatment were also found (<htcite2>).

This diet related to marine microalgae is an important fact to highlight because the inhabitants of Monte Verde were about 90 kilometers (55.22 miles) from the coast (<htcite3>).

source.text: Waters, M. R., & Stafford, T. W., Jr.
Dillehay, T. D., Ramírez, C., Pino, M., Collins, M. B., Rossen, J., & Pino-Navarro, J. D.
Dillehay, T. D., Ramírez, C., Pino, M., Collins, M. B., Rossen, J., & Pino-Navarro, J. D.
Dillehay, T. D., Ramírez, C., Pino, M., Collins, M. B., Rossen, J., & Pino-Navarro, J. D.

history.text:
Reflection 7: Inheritance or common conclusion

When we studied the Jōmon culture (), we observed that this Japanese culture lived, for millennia, basically by gathering.

Despite the Jōmon living on an island, and the inhabitants of Monte Verde living on the continent, for both peoples, marine food was part of the diet.

How would you explain this convergence of habits?

Can you imagine other situations where peoples disconnected for millennia could develop the same habits without needing a direct connection between them?

Given these collections and datings, we can infer some things, which are exposed on the Monte Verde Foundation website itself (<htcite0>):
- About 14,500 years ago, our species, *Homo sapiens*, arrived at Monte Verde;
- About 13,000 years ago, the Clovis culture established itself in the north of the continent.

Since going back in time is ruled out, Clovis could not be the origin of all the continent's inhabitants.

But in science, we always allow ourselves to reflect.

#### There are truths truer than truth

In the year <htdate0>, a new study was published arguing that the periods mentioned for Monte Verde were incorrect, even though these numbers appear in different studies. According to the authors of the new study, Monte Verde should have been inhabited in more recent times, around <htdate1> and <htdate2> (<htcite1>).

This article, published with new evaluations of the Monte Verde dates, was quickly questioned by other scientists, who pointed out errors in it (<htcite2>).

source.text: Surovell, T. A., Méndez, C., García, J.-L., Lüthgens, C., Thompson, J. M., & Latorre, C.

history.text:
Reflection 8: Results that generate research

Did you know that scientific study allows work to be questioned?

The fact that studies present results that other researchers can repeat allows knowledge to be reinforced or refuted. Do you think that is one of the reasons people don't understand science?

Could it be that this misunderstanding occurs because science seems to be something done only in the field or in the laboratory, when, in practice, people do science even in their own kitchen?

Returning to the contradictions between Monte Verde and Clovis: were you able to perceive, in the articles we studied, that the fact that Monte Verde is older than Clovis does not exclude the possibility that people who lived in Clovis migrated to the south of the continent?

Suppose errors do occur in some research — and that is not impossible. Does the fact that the research can be corrected help both the researchers who correct and those who made mistakes to reinforce their knowledge?

The universe is complex, and to understand it, time and dedication are needed, something that many of us do not have. Debates, when conducted in good faith and based on measurements and facts, help bring about a scientific consensus that explains how something happened.

This existing divergence among researchers is a beautiful example of this. We will have a lot to learn from the research that will emerge motivated by what has already occurred. And thanks to the efforts of these researchers, we will be able to better understand how we populated the continent millennia ago.

To end the Monte Verde-Clovis subject more softly, we can again turn to the maxim taught to us by Socrates: these divergences show us that we still have much to learn.
The period in which Monte Verde was occupied demonstrates that there was more than one generation; therefore, this person must have existed (<htcite0>).
The period in which Monte Verde was occupied demonstrates that there was more than one generation; therefore, this person must have existed (<htcite0>).

Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) control region studies have shown that human migrations from Asia to *Abya Yala* (America) occurred between 30.000 and 22.000 años (<htcite0>). With this, we need to reflect on another scenario:

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
Monte Verde has human evidence dating back more than 14,000 years ().

The Clovis occupation occurred more than 12,500 years ago ().

Since genetics shows us a separation older than both sites (), what happened between 22,000 and 14,000 years ago?

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.
Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text: New studies carried out at the Debra L. Friedkin site in Texas, United States of America, and published from <htdate0>, onward, showed that humans inhabited the Buttermilk Creek Complex, according to the most conservative estimates, around <htdate1> and <htdate2> (<htcite0>).

sources.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
#### The haplogroup

Although we have not found any specific mention of the haplogroup of the inhabitants of the Folsom region, we follow the conclusion of the study by Fagundes, N. J. R. *et al.* to assign haplogroup X2a to this person (<htcite0>).

#### Tools

During excavations carried out at the site, 100,000 rock artifacts were found, 328 of which were tools, including 12 complete tools (<htcite1>; <htcite2>).

The tools found at the site are light and small, ideal for scenarios with high mobility (<htcite3>).

#### The challenge of dating

Different approximate dates were mentioned in this text based on data from different articles. However, possibly the way scientists reach these conclusions may not be clear; therefore, let us reflect on this.

source.text: Fagundes, N. J. R. et al.

history.text:
Reflection 10: How many years... ?

When we measure ages, generally speaking, we count from an event, such as birth dates or marriage dates.

When a scientist measures an age, he cannot ask a rock or a fossil for any information. So how do they manage to do this?

Reflection 10 deserves an answer before we continue. When we have organic matter in the environment, tests like Carbon-14 can help us measure the age of fossils. This is possible because the amount of Carbon-14 in an organism decreases over time in a known way (<htcite0>). However, as pointed out by another article investigating the Buttermilk Creek Complex, this was not possible (<htcite1>).

Given this scenario, the researchers opted to use OSL (*Optically Stimulated Luminescence*), a measurement based on the amount of energy accumulated in a rock (crystalline structure), to measure the time since that rock's last exposure to sunlight (<htcite2>; <htcite3>).

#### Expanding the model

The findings at the Buttermilk Creek Complex, as demonstrated by the authors, do not contradict human migration to Monte Verde. On the contrary, this new model connects previous knowledge, estimating human migration at least 16,000 years ago and not ruling out more than one migration (**Figure 9**) (<htcite4>).
Figure 9: Possible human migration across the continent. © Michael R. Water *et al* and Science Magazine (). Shared under Open Access license.

When studying this archaeological site, we saw that previous models do not necessarily need to be discarded to better understand an event such as human migration. With new discoveries, it was even possible to improve the model, for example, by adding more than one migration from Asia.
The number of tools found at the archaeological site and the period in which they were found show that this person existed (<htcite0>).
The number of tools found at the archaeological site and the period in which they were found show that this person existed (<htcite0>).

exercise_v2.question: Is the model “Clovis first” an unquestionable absolute truth?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: We must be careful when classifying something as “truth”, because this conclusion may end up omitting information that allows us to get closer to the facts. It may seem strange, but truth is something much more complex than just limiting two opposing views (truth vs. lie, right vs. wrong). Given this scenario, today we know that the Clovis First model is not the only one that explains the peopling of the Americas. New data show that the peopling was more complex than previously imagined.

exercise_v2.question: Was there development of different tools thousands of years ago in the continent of Abya Yala (America)?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: When observing ancient cultures like Clovis, we observe the existence of points used in hunting. Over the years, when studying the Folsom culture, it is possible to observe that in addition to points, other tools such as the atlatl and knives were developed.

exercise_v2.question: As we make new discoveries, do we better understand how something occurs or occurred?
exercise_v2.additionalInfo: As can be observed in different archaeological sites studied in this text, we went from a model in which all indigenous people spread from one region in a given period, to a broader model that contemplated more than one possible migration. This new model, moreover, encompassed the previous model, making it a particular case.
